title: The Tears of the Magdalene
subtitle: A sinful woman washes the feet of Christ in the house of Simon

stillness: Of the penance of Mary, Gregory says it is better to weep than to speak.
So be still, and let her tears speak.
Whose breast, even of stone, would those tears not soften?
gospel: After this Jesus went up into Judea,
and a certain Pharisee, Simon the leper,
wishing to justify himself, asked the Lord Jesus to eat with him.
And entering the house of the Pharisee, He reclined at table.
And a woman who was a sinner, having heard of it,
came with an alabaster box of ointment;
and standing behind, at His feet,
she began to wash His feet with her tears,
and wiped them with the hairs of her head,
and kissed His feet, and anointed them with the ointment.
behold: Hearing this, Magdalene — who had perhaps already heard Him preaching —
was touched with sorrow of heart within, for her sins,
and fully and perfectly contrite over them,
and kindled with the fire of His love,
took the alabaster, that vessel of ointment, and went on to the place of the banquet.
Seeing that Jesus had cured the leper Simon,
she trusted that He would as easily cleanse the uncleanness of her soul.
With her face bowed down and her eyes cast to the ground,
passing before the guests, she did not rest until she came to Jesus.
Then at once, standing beside His feet, she fell prostrate —
for she dared not place herself before Him, for the shame of her sins —
and sinking her face down upon those very feet,
for already she loved Him intensely and above all things, and burned in His love,
she began to pour forth mightily in weeping and sobbing,
and with tears running down in plenty to bathe and wash the feet of the Lord.
Here the greatness of her contrition is plain:
her penance, pouring out the blood of her heart, wept so much
that her tears could wash the feet of a man.
And ceasing at last from weeping, with her own hair she wiped His washed feet,
and, as love grew, kissed them often, sweetly and lovingly, and could not be satisfied.
And because His feet were soiled and weary from the road,
she anointed them with precious ointment,
to cool their heat and soften their pain from the labor of the journey.
And these things, which were done outwardly —
what her intention turned over within was far more fervent, which God alone beheld.
consider: By this sinful woman is signified every soul truly penitent and contrite over its sins.
As she offended God by guilt, so let her serve Him by penance.
In that the woman stands behind, devotion and humility are recognized.
In that she bathes His feet with tears, true penance and compunction of heart are shown.
And because she hides and wipes away those same tears with her hair,
she shows herself to be none of those who do their works to be seen by men.
Consider, says Gregory, what she did, and she would not moderate what she did.
She entered in upon the banqueters; she came unbidden;
among the feasting, she offered her tears.
Learn with what grief she burns, who is not ashamed to weep even among the feast.
Because she looked upon the tears of her own foulness,
she ran to the fountain of mercy to be washed, and did not blush before the guests.
For because she gravely blushed for herself within,
she believed there was nothing she need be ashamed of without.
We too are that woman, if with our whole heart we return to the Lord after sin,
and imitate the mourning of her penance.
What she had shamefully shown to herself, this she now offered praiseworthily to God.
prayer: Lord Jesus, what she shamefully used for the odor of her flesh,
this she now offered to Thee in praise.
With my eyes I have sought Thee upon the earth.
Touched with sorrow of heart within, I run, contrite, to the fountain of Thy mercy.
I dare not set myself before Thee, for the shame of my sins;
behind, beside Thy feet, I take my place,
for I follow now the footsteps of Him whom I once assailed.
Wash me with the tears Thou hast given;
and let me not be satisfied with loving Thee.

latinJewel.latin: Cujus enim vel saxeum pectus, illæ hujus peccatricis mulieris lacrymæ, ad exemplum pœnitendi non emolliant?
latinJewel.english: Whose breast, though it were of stone, would the tears of this sinful woman not soften, as a pattern of penance?
